elements.sent-2411-001: This paper presents ETA-IK, a novel Execution-Time-Aware Inverse Kinematics method tailored for dual-arm robotic systems.
elements.sent-2411-002: The primary goal is to optimize motion execution time by leveraging the redundancy of both arms, specifically in tasks where only the relative pose of the robots is constrained, such as dual-arm scanning of unknown objects.
elements.sent-2411-003: Unlike traditional inverse kinematics methods that use surrogate metrics such as joint configuration distance, our method incorporates direct motion execution time and implicit collisions into the optimization process, thereby finding target joints that allow subsequent trajectory generation to get more efficient and collision-free motion.
elements.sent-2411-004: A neural network based execution time approximator is employed to predict time-efficient joint configurations while accounting for potential collisions.
elements.sent-2411-005: Through experimental evaluation on a system composed of a UR5 and a KUKA iiwa robot, we demonstrate significant reductions in execution time.
elements.sent-2411-006: The proposed method outperforms conventional approaches, showing improved motion efficiency without sacrificing positioning accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-007: These results highlight the potential of ETA-IK to improve the performance of dual-arm systems in applications, where efficiency and safety are paramount.
elements.sent-2411-008: While robots are becoming increasingly capable of performing everyday tasks in a Human-Robot Collaboration setting, there are still many situations where robots must operate without humans present in the environment.
elements.sent-2411-009: Notable examples include dismantling nuclear facilities, disaster relief operations, and tasks in human-hazardous environments \cite{woock_robdekon_2022 \cite{petereit_robdekon_2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: In such environments, the criteria of human likeness and the predictability of motion, which are essential for collaborative tasks, are of less importance than efficiency, which represents the gold standard.
elements.sent-2411-011: Our work originates from a scenario involving the scanning and modeling of unknown objects during the dismantling and decontamination of a nuclear facility.
elements.sent-2411-012: To be specific, robots have to efficiently coordinate with each other to conduct rapid surface modeling and reconstruction of a unknown object.
elements.sent-2411-013: With the sophisticated reconstructed 3D model, a close-range radiation scanning over the surface of the unknown object is then possible.
elements.sent-2411-014: The focus of this work is the motion generation for rapid modeling.
elements.sent-2411-015: For the modeling task, the robots traverse over a given sequence of object-centric perception angles, e.g. generated from \ac{NBV methods \cite{pan_scvp_2022 \cite{kobayashi_obtaining_2023 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: At least one robot should manipulate and then hold the object for perception while another robot with perception instruments attached should position itself to approach the predefined perception angle, i.e. to keep a relative pose including position and orientation to the first robot.
elements.sent-2411-017: As the perception completes, the both robots move on to the next perception angles in the sequence.
elements.sent-2411-018: Note that the perception angles, i.e. the relative pose between the robots, only imposes a constraint of six \ac{DoF , leaving us possibility to leverage redundancy of the robot arms to optimize the joint configurations for higher efficiency, e.g. shorter time traversing from one perception pose to the next one in the given sequence.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Figures/first_page_with_robot.png} \caption{Improving execution time for modeling radioactive unknown objects with two robot arms (top left). Given a set of relative poses representing the scan poses (bottom left), ETA-IK aims to use the capabilities of this highly redundant system to solve the IK problem and find the optimal and collision-free joint configurations (top right), to accelerate the scanning process (bottom right). } \label{fig:pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-019: In this work, we use the motion execution time traversing from the current robot configuration to the target pose as a metric to characterize the efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-020: The goal of the this work is to compute the target joint configurations that can reach the target pose while keeping the execution time as short as possible.
elements.sent-2411-021: This metric can be integrated into a multi-objective inverse kinematics framework.
elements.sent-2411-022: Some inverse kinematics algorithms use difference between joint configurations as surrogate loss \cite{trutman_globally_2022 \cite{votroubek_globally_2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: This surrogate loss becomes less representative if the point-to-point motion from the current robot joint configuration to the computed target configuration is not collision-free.
elements.sent-2411-024: This is a common phenomenon in the dual-arm setting.
elements.sent-2411-025: On the other hand, we use a learned neural network to directly provide an estimation of the motion execution time, taking into account the detour to avoid collision, resulting in a more accurate and representative metric.
elements.sent-2411-026: The main contributions of this work are as follows,.
elements.sent-2411-027: A multi-objective \ac{IK method for dual-arm setting that formulates the problem using relative pose between TCPs of the robots, optimizing simultaneously over the redundancy of both arms.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Improving motion execution efficiency by directly including motion execution time into the IK optimization problem, showing significant advantage compared to the methods that use surrogate terms such as joint distance.
elements.sent-2411-029: An execution time approximator that takes into account potential risks of collision, providing more accurate estimation regarding execution time of a collision-free point-to-point motion generated by standard \ac{TG given the the start and target configurations.}
elements.subsubsection-2411-001: Explore Redundancy Resolution for Redundant Robot and Dual-arm System
elements.sent-2411-030: Redundant robots are robotic systems that have more \ac{DoF than is strictly necessary to perform a given task, and for regular reach a pose tasks, robots with more than six \ac{DoF have redundancy.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Numerous studies have leveraged redundancy resolution in seven \ac{DoF robots to enhance motion optimization, such as introducing joint limit constraints\cite{flacco_motion_2012 , improving manipulability to avoid singularities\cite{vahrenkamp_representing_2015 , enhancing stiffness \cite{tang_towards_2023 , avoiding obstacles\cite{jiang_obstacle_2019 , or reducing energy consumption\cite{dietrich_overview_2015 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: When the objective is to achieve coordinated relative poses between multiple robotic arms, the nullspace of the relative Jacobian becomes larger, enabling further optimization across multiple sub-tasks or asymmetric tasks \cite{su_manipulability_2019 \cite{tarbouriech_dual-arm_2018 .}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Some research has extended the concept of the nullspace by incorporating advanced optimization strategies.
elements.sent-2411-034: For instance, cost functions based on the manipulability of key waypoints have been proposed to define secondary tasks \cite{faroni_global_2016 , thereby optimizing the average manipulability index throughout the task execution.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Additionally, by deriving the nullspace into acceleration domain, nullspace-based impedance controller is proposed to handle force interactive tasks \cite{lee_relative_2014 and minimization of motion time under kinematic/dynamic bounds can also be achieved \cite{al_khudir_faster_2018 .}}
elements.sent-2411-036: To the best of the author's knowledge, there is currently no research that directly incorporates execution time explicitly into the \ac{IK optimization problem.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Some studies \cite{trutman_globally_2022 \cite{votroubek_globally_2024 that aim to find a global optimum introduce a distance cost function specific to the joint configuration.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: By setting a specific angle as the current joint configuration, these methods attempt to minimize joint movements, thereby optimizing execution time.
elements.sent-2411-039: However, achieving a global optimum requires converting the optimization problem into a convex form, which restricts the extensibility of objective function and leads to an exponential increase in computational time as the number of redundant degrees of freedom increases.
elements.sent-2411-040: In addition, other related methods include inertial load optimization based on Cartesian spatial direction and the concepts of pseudo-inverse velocity and pseudo-inverse acceleration\cite{al_khudir_faster_2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-041: These methods are more suitable for local optimization and path following, which differs from the problem addressed in this work.
elements.sent-2411-042: Traditional methods to optimize the execution time are usually incorporated into the trajectory optimization step, after IK solving and path planning.
elements.sent-2411-043: There are many approaches, such as Time Scaling, Time-Optimal Path Parameterization (TOPP) \cite{pham_general_2014 , Time-Optimal Path Parameterization Based on Reachability Analysis (TOPPRA) \cite{pham_new_2017 , and other Time-Optimal Trajectory Generation (TOTG) techniques \cite{berscheid_jerk-limited_2021 \cite{kroger_opening_2011 , are typically applied as post-processing steps rather than being integrated into the single-pose \ac{IK solution.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: While these methods are capable of generating efficient motions, they generally assume that a feasible joint target pose or a series of waypoints is already available.
elements.sent-2411-045: This makes them more appropriate for waypoint-based motion planning, as opposed to addressing single-pose \ac{IK challenges.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Regarding the learning based IK solver, very earlier, the authors in \cite{oyama2001inverse proposed to use a prediction network to reduce the IK solving time.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Numerous studies \cite{vu2023machine \cite{bocsi2011learning \cite{mahajan_solving_nodate have explored various neural network architectures for solving the \ac{IK problem.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Directly learning an IK solution for redundant robots using an end-to-end neural network approach presents significant challenges.
elements.sent-2411-049: IKNET \cite{bensadoun_neural_2022 demonstrated improvements compared to MLP by decreasing the position error into the centimeter level.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Unsupervised learning-based IK is proposed for highly redundant system\cite{stephan_learning_2023 , reducing the error of a 20 \ac{DoF redundant robot to 2 cm when only the position error is considered.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Another important finding is that the neural network solver is capable of implicitly generating collision-free solutions when rained on datasets containing obstacle avoidance scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-052: On the other hand, for the \ac{IK solver based on optimization, the network can be used to estimate some metrics, such as the nullspace parameter \cite{towell_learning_2010 .}}
elements.sent-2411-053: For a dual-arm robotic system with 12 to 14 \ac{DoF s, integrating learning-based methods with optimization has the potential to address the limitations of both approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-054: The primary goal of this work was to find a target joint configuration $\mathbf{q _t$ for a \ac{PTP minimum motion time \ac{IK problem with a given initial configuration $\mathbf{q _0$, a desired target \ac{TCP pose $\mathbf{p _t$ (relative pose between 2 \ac{TCP s $\mathbf{p _{r,t}$ for a dual-arm system), zero initial/final joint velocity $\dot{\mathbf{q _0=\dot{\mathbf{q _t=0$, and robot dynamic and kinematic limits as follow: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathbf{q}_{\text{min}} \leq \mathbf{q}(t) \leq \mathbf{q}_{\text{max}}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}_{\text{min}} \leq \dot{\mathbf{q}}(t) \leq \dot{\mathbf{q}}_{\text{max}}, \ddot{\mathbf{q}}_{\text{min}} \leq \ddot{\mathbf{q}}(t) \leq \ddot{\mathbf{q}}_{\text{max}} \forall t \in [0, t_{\text{end}}]. \end{aligned}\end{equation} The primary motivation for formulating this problem is to optimize the process of scanning unknown objects as efficiently as possible using the \ac{NBV method.}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: In a dual-arm robotic system, where one arm holds the unknown object and the other is equipped with a scanner or camera, the \ac{NBV is defined by the relative pose of the two \ac{TCP s.}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Given the redundancy of the robotic system, there exists an infinite number of configurations that can satisfy the relative pose constraints.
elements.sent-2411-057: To identify the most efficient \ac{IK solution for this motion task, the distance between the initial and target configurations can serve as a potential metric for estimating motion time.}
elements.sent-2411-058: However, this approach is complicated by the fact that most robots have unique joint limits, making it challenging to predict motion time based solely on configuration distance.
elements.sent-2411-059: And the estimated joint distance in the \ac{IK phase can not explicitly illustrate the actual execution time due to the collision avoidance behaviour considered in the post-processing phase.}
elements.sent-2411-060: As mentioned above, most time-optimization methods for motion execution apply post-processing to target configurations or joint waypoints after they are determined.
elements.sent-2411-061: It remains an open question regarding how to explore the solution space of a single inverse kinematics (IK) problem to obtain a configuration that optimizes execution time directly.
elements.sent-2411-062: The closest approach mentioned in \cite{al_khudir_faster_2018 models the problem as a nonlinear programming problem and employs numerical methods based on direct collocation to minimize execution time.}
elements.sent-2411-063: While this approach calculates the optimal trajectory simultaneously with the execution time, it introduces significant computational overhead.
elements.sent-2411-064: With the six \ac{DoF perception poses generated by the NBV algorithms, we leverage redundancy of the dual arms to optimize the joint configurations for fast execution.}
elements.sent-2411-065: First, we use the relative pose between both arms to formulate an inverse kinematics problem.
elements.sent-2411-066: Then, we convert this formulation into a parallel multi-objectives problem and incorporate execution time directly in the objectives.
elements.sent-2411-067: During the optimization, the joint configurations converge to the target desired pose and sequentially improve the execution time.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{Figures/Pipeline_fixed.png} \caption{Pipeline of ETA-IK: given a desired perception pose, ETA-IK first generate a batch of initial configurations, highlighted with yellow. And then, a multilayer-perceptron time apporiximator and pose error objectives are used for parallel optimization, highlighted with blue. After $N$ iterations, the results is a batch of joint configurations. We select the best one according to criterion such as execution time and relative pose error between two robots, highlighted with green.} \label{fig:pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-068: In a dual-arm robot system, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:real_robot_experiment_setup , one robot, called the tool robot, holds the tool (camera or scanner), while the other arm, called the reference robot, holds the object to be modelled.}
elements.sent-2411-069: The concept of relative Jacobian is built on vectors that represent the position and orientation of the \ac{TCP of the tool robot relative to the \ac{TCP of the reference robot.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: It imposes a constraint regarding the the relative motion between the two \ac{TCP s without any restrictions regarding of their absolute positions.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Essentially, by using this Jacobian, the focus in a two-arm maneuver is the relative motion between the \ac{TCP .}
elements.sent-2411-072: The relative position vector $\mathbf{x _{R}$ is then defined as: \begin{equation}\mathbf{x}_R = \mathbf{x}_B - \mathbf{x}_A\end{equation} where $\mathbf{x _A, \mathbf{x _B \in \mathbb{R ^{n_R}$ are the \ac{TCP pose of the reference robot and the tool robot, respectively and $n_R$ refers to the \ac{DoF s of the robotic arms.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: By differentiating this equation with respect to time, we obtain: \begin{equation}\dot{\mathbf{x}}_R = \dot{\mathbf{x}}_B - \dot{\mathbf{x}}_A = J_R \dot{\mathbf{q}}\end{equation} where $\dot{\mathbf{x _R$ represents the relative velocity between two robots' \ac{TCP s and $\dot{\mathbf{x _B, \dot{\mathbf{x _A$ denote the absolute velocity of the respective robot's \ac{TCP .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: The relative Jacobian $J_R \in \mathbb{R ^{n_R \times n_T}$ can be expressed in terms of the individual Jacobians of the robots \cite{lee_relative_2014 : \begin{equation}J_R = \begin{bmatrix} -R_{RA} J_A R_{RT}^T R_{RB} J_B \end{bmatrix}\end{equation} where $J_A \in \mathbb{R ^{n_R \times n_A}$ and $J_B \in \mathbb{R ^{n_R \times n_B}$ are the Jacobians of robots A and B, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-075: The term $n_{[\cdot]}$ denotes the \ac{DoF of a robot and $n_T = n_A + n_B$ is the total number of \ac{DoF s for the system.}}
elements.sent-2411-076: The block diagonal rotation matrices $R_{RA , R_{RT , R_{RB \in \mathbb{R ^{n_R \times n_R}$ are determined with respect to their associated reference frames.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: The relative Jacobian $J_R$ provide gradients of the pose error in the multi-objective optimization.
elements.sent-2411-078: The primary objective for the defined scenario is to accurately determine the relative pose between the reference robot and the tool robot.
elements.sent-2411-079: To achieve this, the first components of the objective function are defined to address both the relative position and orientation errors.
elements.sent-2411-080: For the relative position, the objective function can be formulated as: \begin{equation}\chi_p(\mathbf{q}) = \| (p_{B,g} - p_{A,g}) - (FK_B(\mathbf{q}_B) - FK_A(\mathbf{q}_A) \|^2 \label{eq:position}\end{equation} where $FK_A(\mathbf{q _A)$ and $FK_B(\mathbf{q _B)$ are the forward kinematics functions of robots A and B given their joint configurations.}}
elements.sent-2411-081: For the relative orientation error, the objective function is given by: \begin{equation}\chi_o(\mathbf{q}) = \text{d}( \text{d}(q_{B,g}, q_{A,g}), \text{d}(q_B[FK(\mathbf{q}_B)], q_A[FK(\mathbf{q}_A)]) ), \label{eq:orientation}\end{equation} where $d(\cdot)$ is the displacement between two quatenions, and note that $\mathbf{q}$ refers joint configuration of the robot, and $q$ represents quaternions.
elements.sent-2411-082: To represent the motion time, a commonly used surrogate metric is the distance between the starting and target joint configurations: \begin{equation}\chi_{\text{mt}}(\mathbf{q}) =\| \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_0 \|^2,\end{equation} where $\mathbf{q}$ is the joint configuration being optimized.
elements.sent-2411-083: In this formulation, a bang-bang control strategy can be assumed at the velocity level, and the distance metric is normalized by introducing a weighting factor to account for the different velocity limits of each joint. \begin{equation}\chi_{\text{mt}}(\mathbf{q}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n_T} w_i \left| q_i - q_{i,0} \right|^2. \label{eq:distance}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-084: Inverse kinematics optimization aiming for global optimality is significant computationally expensive.
elements.sent-2411-085: Recent study \cite{votroubek_globally_2024 reports an average computation time of 77.6 seconds to optimize a system with 10 DoF using the similar objective functions in \ref{subsec: multi-obj-ik .}}
elements.sent-2411-086: The optimizing problems of a dual-arm system involves commonly 12-14 \ac{DoF , resulting in an expanded optimization space.}
elements.sent-2411-087: Due to the curse of dimensionality, this increase significantly elevates computational expense, rendering such methods impractical.
elements.sent-2411-088: The other non-convex optimization algorithms such as \cite{rakita_relaxedik_2018 , trade global optimality for potentially faster computation.}
elements.sent-2411-089: To improve the chances of finding a better solution, a parallel optimization approach is often employed \cite{sundaralingam_curobo_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-090: This method uses multiple initial guesses in parallel, and the best result is selected as the optimal solution.
elements.sent-2411-091: Since the mapping relationship between Cartesian coordinates and the joints of a redundant manipulator is not unique, the number and distribution of initial guesses can significantly influence the final optimization result.
elements.sent-2411-092: The entire objective function is \begin{equation}f(\mathbf{q}) = w_p \cdot \chi_p(\mathbf{q}) + w_o \cdot\chi_o(\mathbf{q}) + w_mt \cdot \chi_{\text{mt}}(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{q}_0)+ w_b \cdot \chi_{b}(\mathbf{q})\end{equation} where $w_i$ represent the weight for the corresponding objective terms, and $\chi_{b (\mathbf{q )$ is the joint limit cost proposed in \cite{sundaralingam_curobo_2023 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: Initially, Halton sampling is employed to generate samples from the configuration space that are free of collisions, serving as initial guesses for the optimization process.
elements.sent-2411-094: The step directions for the optimization are then computed based on the gradient of the defined objective function.
elements.sent-2411-095: Once the step direction is determined, a line search is conducted.
elements.sent-2411-096: This involves scaling the step direction with a discrete set of magnitudes \cite{sundaralingam_curobo_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-097: The optimal magnitude from this set is selected using the Armijo and Wolfe conditions, ensuring that the step size is sufficient for descent and satisfies the curvature condition, which is crucial for avoiding overshooting and ensuring stable convergence in \ac{IK problems.}
elements.sent-2411-098: After several iterations, all optimization results are verified for feasibility and convergence to ensure that the calculated \ac{IK solutions reside in a collision-free joint space and meet the predefined position and rotation error thresholds.}
elements.sent-2411-099: A solution is considered successful if it satisfies both conditions.
elements.sent-2411-100: If a solution does not meet these criteria, an offset is added to the evaluation metric when selecting the best solution.
elements.sent-2411-101: Typically, the \ac{IK problem defines the optimal solution as the one that minimizes the pose error.}
elements.sent-2411-102: Since our method also consider the motion time, the metric for selecting the optimal solution can be defined in several ways: it can prioritize the pose error, the motion time, or a combination of both.
elements.sent-2411-103: This flexibility allows the optimization to balance accuracy in achieving the desired pose with the efficiency of motion, depending on the specific requirements of the task.
elements.sent-2411-104: Using surrogate loss such as distance between joint configurations as optimization objectives assumes that resultant point-to-point motion between the start and target configuration is collision-free and executable.
elements.sent-2411-105: For a dual-arm setting, this assumption is usually not legitimate and therefore a post-processing step such as path planning is needed.
elements.sent-2411-106: This additional step introduce an gab between the surrogate loss and the actual execution time.
elements.sent-2411-107: Directly coping execution time in the optimization does not has such problem.
elements.sent-2411-108: For dual arms mounted on a fixed base, we can deploy offline optimal path planning or trajectory optimization algorithms to compute the collision-free motion between two arbitrary joint configuration and at the end acquire the actual execution time.
elements.sent-2411-109: However, this paradigm can not be included into the multi-objective optimization due to its computation complexity.
elements.sent-2411-110: Instead, we collect data from these offline methods and use a multilayer perceptron (MLP) $f_\theta$ to approximate the execution time during the optimization.
elements.sent-2411-111: Previous works \cite{huang2024planning show that MLPs are capable to capture the latent relation between joint configurations and time.}
elements.sent-2411-112: Furthermore, MLPs can not only achieve fast inference time during optimization, but also support batch operations and provide gradients for the optimization.
elements.sent-2411-113: In practice, we use positional encoding $\mathbf{q _{PE}$ = [$\mathbf{q}$, $\cos\mathbf{q}$, $\sin\mathbf{q}$] as input for the approximator.}
elements.sent-2411-114: Given the start and target configuration $\mathbf{q _0$, $\mathbf{q _t$, the approximator outputs \begin{equation}\hat{t} = f_\theta(\mathbf{q}_{PE,0}, \mathbf{q}_{PE,t}).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-115: Literature \cite{koptev2022neural}\cite{mildenhall2021Nerf suggests that this can improve the expressiveness of the MLP.}
elements.sent-2411-116: The experimental setup for the dual-arm system, shown in \ref{fig:real_robot_experiment_setup , consists of a UR5 and a KUKA LBR iiwa 14 R820 robot, with a combined total of 13 \ac{DoF .}}
elements.sent-2411-117: The KUKA iiwa is designated for object grasping due to its higher load capacity, while a high-resolution 3D lidar Robin W from Seyond is mounted on the UR5's end-effector for scanning purposes.
elements.sent-2411-118: Given a start and target configuration, the time approximator provides an estimated execution time with implicit consideration of potential collision risk.
elements.sent-2411-119: To train the time approximator, we generate 250,000 collision-free start and target pairs.
elements.sent-2411-120: Then, we feed these pairs to the TOPPRA \cite{pham_new_2017 trajectory generator and Curobo TrajOpt \cite{sundaralingam_curobo_2023 to collect respective execution time.}}
elements.sent-2411-121: The difference between these two trajectory generators is that, the one from Curobo takes collision into account and always returns collision-free trajectories while the TG methods can return trajectories that are not executable.
elements.sent-2411-122: In our generated dataset, approximately 50\% of the trajectories generated by TOPPRA are not collision-free and, therefore, not executable in practice.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.49\textwidth]{Figures/Experiment_setup.pdf} \caption{Scanning and modeling with two different manipulators: The KUKA iiwa robot picks up the target object (stone) and the UR5 robot carries a 3D lidar scanner. To get a complete and accurate model, both robots have to change the scanning pose through many relative poses that are projected as the best scanning perspective. This process needs to be accelerated by using our proposed approach.} \label{fig:real_robot_experiment_setup}
elements.sent-2411-123: In the simulation, a general evaluation of the proposed approach is conducted.
elements.sent-2411-124: This involves generating a random collision-free initial joint configuration $\mathbf{q _0$ and a random relative Cartesian pose $\mathbf{p _{R,t}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-125: A random reference target joint configuration $\mathbf{q _t$ is sampled, and the absolute \ac{TCP poses of the two robots are then calculated individually.}}
elements.sent-2411-126: For comparative analysis, three baselines are provided.
elements.sent-2411-127: These baselines utilize the random reference $\mathbf{q _t$ directly as the endpoint, perform optimization on Eq. (\ref{eq:distance ) based on absolute and relative Cartesian pose as motion time term in the objective function.}}
elements.sent-2411-128: The resulting motion time based on the \ac{IK solutions is subsequently calculated using the TOPPRA algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-129: The robot joint limits used when generating the dataset for the network align with those in TOPPRA, including velocity $\left\{3.15, 3.15, 3.15, 3.2, 3.2, 3.2\right\}$ and $\left\{10.0, 10.0, 10.0, 10.0, 10.0, 10.0, 10.0\right\}$ $\frac{rad {s}$, acceleration $\left\{5.0, 5.0, 3.0, 2.0, 2.0, 2.0\right\}, \left\{5.0, 5.0, 3.0, 2.0, 2.0, 2.0, 2.0 \right\}$ $\frac{rad {s^{2 }$, and jerk constraints for UR5 and IIWA respectively $\left\{500.0, 500.0, 500.0, 500.0, 500.0, 500.0\right\}$, $\left\{500.0, 500.0, 500.0, 500.0, 500.0, 500.0, 500.0\right\}$ $\frac{rad {s^{3 }$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-130: All optimization parameters are summarized in Tab.. \ref{tab:exp_1 .}
elements.sent-2411-131: To explore the optimal solution, the number of initial guesses and optimization iterations are set to a high value, namely $4096$ initial guesses and $500$ iterations.
elements.sent-2411-132: This ensures a comprehensive evaluation of the solution space.
elements.sent-2411-133: The weights for position and orientation error are $2000, 2500$ and the execution time weights are set as $250, 500$ for joint distance and execution time approximator, respectively.

elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{Figures/simulation.pdf} \caption{Execution time comparison between reference, method (C) and (G) for collision-free trajectory generation: The starting pose and the reference target shown are randomly generated. The starting joint configuration, reference target configuration, and the IK solution generated by method (C) and (G) in Tab. \ref{tab:exp_2} are illustrated with the corresponding position and velocity profile over time generated by TrajOpt.} \label{fig:simulation}

elements.sent-2411-134: The execution time results indicate that using absolute Cartesian coordinates as position targets is less effective than relative positions for execution time optimization.
elements.sent-2411-135: The distance loss (C in the Tab.
elements.sent-2411-136: It is worth noting that due to the greater optimization space, the positional error of (E) is significantly smaller.
elements.sent-2411-137: If the solution to success is sorted only by execution time cost, the solution obtained by proposed ETA-IK is almost $25\%$ shorter than the execution time of the randomly generated ref configuration, performs best among all comparison methods.
elements.sent-2411-138: For the approximator trained by the collision-free dataset, the \ac{NBV candidate poses are utilized as target relative poses.}
elements.sent-2411-139: Since these scanned positions are object-centered, they are more prone to collisions, thereby highlighting the influence of the collision-aware approximator on the \ac{IK solution.}
elements.sent-2411-141: Method (C), which employs relative position and distance loss as objectives, results in minimal joint changes (A typical example with random sampled starting and target is shown in Fig. \ref{fig:simulation ).}
elements.sent-2411-142: However, due to the need for collision avoidance, the final collision-free trajectory execution time is longer than method (G).
elements.sent-2411-143: The symmetry of the velocity profile indicates the presence of obstacle avoidance behavior.
elements.sent-2411-144: While the reference trajectory is planned without encountering obstacles, resulting in a completely symmetrical velocity profile.
elements.sent-2411-145: The longer joint movement distances increased the execution time.
elements.sent-2411-146: In contrast, the IK solution obtained through the proposed approximator optimization exhibits relatively less obstacle avoidance behavior and achieves optimal execution time among the three methods considered.
elements.sent-2411-147: The real robot experiments validated the motion generation pipeline, demonstrating that the generated trajectories perform effectively both in the simulation environment and on real robots.
elements.sent-2411-148: A video of the experiment is attached to provide a visual demonstration of the system’s performance.
elements.sent-2411-149: In this work, we presented ETA-IK, an Execution-Time-Aware Inverse Kinematics method for dual-arm systems, with a specific application in scanning unknown objects during the dismantling of a nuclear facility.
elements.sent-2411-150: Our primary focus was on optimizing motion execution time by leveraging the redundancy of dual-arm systems.
elements.sent-2411-151: The results demonstrated that integrating motion execution time directly into the IK optimization framework yields significant improvements in efficiency compared to traditional methods that rely on surrogate metrics, such as joint distance.
elements.sent-2411-152: The experiments showed that our proposed execution time approximator effectively improves the overall system performance.
elements.sent-2411-153: By incorporating collision avoidance into the optimization process, our approach could account for self-collision without introducing substantial computational overhead.
elements.sent-2411-154: This was particularly evident in both simulated and real-world scenarios, where the system was able to achieve more efficient execution while maintaining the relative pose between the two robots.
elements.sent-2411-155: In addition, we have identified several limitations in this work.
elements.sent-2411-156: One is the additional inference time required for the neural network-based approximator compared to simple distance-based metrics.
elements.sent-2411-157: However, this drawback is mitigated by the overall improvement in the motion execution time.
elements.sent-2411-158: Future work could focus on reducing the computational overhead of the approximator and exploring the application of this approach to multi-robot systems with higher degrees of freedom.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
elements.sent-2411-159: The primary goal is to optimize motion execution time by leveraging the redundancy of the entire system, specifically in tasks where only the relative pose of the robots is constrained, such as dual-arm scanning of unknown objects.
elements.sent-2411-160: Unlike traditional IK methods using surrogate metrics, our approach directly optimizes execution time while implicitly considering collisions.
elements.sent-2411-161: While robots are becoming increasingly capable of performing everyday tasks in Human-Robot Collaboration settings, there are still many scenarios where robots must operate autonomously, without humans present.
elements.sent-2411-162: Notable examples include the dismantling of nuclear facilities, disaster relief operations, and tasks conducted in hazardous environments \cite{petereit_robdekon_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-163: In these environments, factors such as human-like motion and predictable behavior, which are crucial for human-robot collaboration, become secondary.
elements.sent-2411-164: Instead, execution efficiency and collision avoidance become the primary concerns.
elements.sent-2411-165: As shown in Fig. \ref{fig:pipeline1 , this work originates from a real-world scenario involving the scanning and modeling of unknown objects during the dismantling and decontamination of a nuclear facility.}
elements.sent-2411-166: Robots must efficiently coordinate to achieve rapid 3D modeling and proceed further manipulation such as radioactive scanning and wiping over the object based on this 3D model.
elements.sent-2411-167: Given a sequence of discrete object-centric perception poses (e.g. generated with \ac{NBV methods \cite{pan_scvp_2022 ), this work focuses on finding the joint configurations of a dual-arm system for each pose with an objective to minimize the execution time of the object modeling process.}}
elements.sent-2411-168: Compared to continuous scanning, discrete perception poses allows for more time-efficient execution, as the robots can move between viewpoints with full acceleration and velocity capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-169: Additionally, the motion between two robotic arms enables faster execution than a single arm, as velocity and acceleration limits can be superimposed across both manipulators.
elements.sent-2411-170: Furthermore, dual-arm systems provide additional \ac{DoF compared to single-arm setups, enabling more flexible solutions that can reduce motion time, avoid self-collisions, and better adapt to task constraints.}
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Figures/first_page_with_robot.png} \caption{Application and Motivation: Improving execution time for modeling radioactive unknown objects with two robot arms (top left). Given a set of relative poses representing the perception poses (bottom left), ETA-IK aims to use the redundant \ac{DoF} to solve each single IK problem and find the optimal and collision-free joint configurations (top right), to accelerate the entire scanning process (bottom right). } \label{fig:pipeline1}
elements.sent-2411-171: In dual-arm manipulation, a particular class of problem arises where only the relative motion of the \ac{EE is specified as input, imposing a path constraint on the motion planning problem \cite{doi:10.1177/0278364920988087 .}}
elements.sent-2411-172: When only the relative \ac{TCP pose between the two arms is important, the redundant \ac{DoF allow for multiple kinematic solutions to achieve the same task.}}
elements.sent-2411-173: Consider a dual-arm manipulation scenario where one arm holds an object while the other utilizes a LiDAR sensor to collect point cloud data (as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:pipeline1 ).}
elements.sent-2411-174: In such cases, the selection of the joint configurations for the given desired relative \ac{TCP poses in Cartesian space determines the execution performance of the task.}
elements.sent-2411-175: The motion efficiency is determined not only by minimizing joint displacement but also by avoiding potential collisions and accounting for varying joint dynamic constraints.
elements.sent-2411-176: Poor \ac{IK selection can result in longer execution times.}
elements.sent-2411-177: Therefore, this paper aims to address a \ac{PTP relative target pose problem, which fundamentally differs from the continuous relative motion problem \cite{doi:10.1177/0278364920988087 .}}
elements.sent-2411-178: To characterize motion efficiency, we define motion execution time—the time required to move from the current robot configuration to the target pose—as our primary metric.
elements.sent-2411-179: The objective of this work is to compute \ac{PTP target joint configurations that satisfy task constraints while minimizing execution time.}
elements.sent-2411-180: The main contributions of this work are:.
elements.sent-2411-181: A neural network-based execution time approximator is developed that accounts for potential self-collisions, providing accurate predictions regarding execution time of a collision-free point-to-point motion generated by standard \ac{TG .}
elements.sent-2411-182: A multi-objective \ac{IK method for dual-arm systems is presented that formulates the problem using relative pose between \ac{TCP s of the robots, optimizing motion efficiency by fully leveraging the system's redundancy.}}
elements.sent-2411-183: The motion efficiency is achieved by directly integrating the execution time approximator into the \ac{IK optimization framework , demonstration significant improvements over traditional approaches that rely on surrogate metrics like joint configuration distance.}
elements.sent-2411-184: Numerous studies have leveraged redundancy resolution in seven \ac{DoF robots to enhance motion optimization \cite{flacco_motion_2012, vahrenkamp_representing_2015, tang_towards_2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-185: Some non-convex multi-objective optimization methods \cite{rakita_relaxedik_2018, rakita_shared_2019 also utilize joint configuration distance as an optimization metric.}
elements.sent-2411-186: Furthermore, evolutionary algorithms \cite{wu_t-ik_2021, 8449979 have been combined with optimization to improve convergence speed.}
elements.sent-2411-187: Although they mentioned \ac{multi-EE applications in their work, but these methods typically solve separate \ac{IK porblems for each \ac{EE .}}}
elements.sent-2411-188: And these methods are more suitable for local optimization and path following, which differs from the problem addressed in this work.
elements.sent-2411-189: Many approaches \cite{pham_general_2014, pham_new_2017, 9905530 are typically applied as post-processing steps rather than being integrated into the single-pose \ac{IK solution.}}
elements.sent-2411-190: They generally assume that a feasible joint target pose or a series of waypoints is already available, which makes them more appropriate for waypoint-based motion planning, as opposed to addressing single-pose \ac{IK challenges.}
elements.sent-2411-191: Numerous studies \cite{vu2023machine, bocsi2011learning have explored various neural network architectures for solving the \ac{IK problem.}}
elements.sent-2411-192: Unsupervised learning-based IK is proposed for highly redundant system\cite{stephan_learning_2023 , achieving the similar accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-193: Another important finding from this work is that the neural network solver is capable of implicitly generating collision-free solutions in static scenes when trained on datasets containing obstacle avoidance scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-194: This work integrates learning-based methods with optimization has the potential to preserve the accuracy of standard approaches while simultaneously enhancing performance metrics that are challenging to model explicitly.
elements.sent-2411-195: The goal of this work is to find a joint configuration $\bm{q = [\bm{q_A , \bm{q_B ] $ of a dual-arm system that results in the desired target relative \ac{TCP pose $\bm{x_R}$ while minimizing the motion execution time $T$ of a trajectory $\bm q_{0:T = [\bm q_0, \dots, \bm q_T]$ connecting $\bm q$ and a given initial configuration $\bm{q _s$, where $\bm{q _0= \bm{q _s, \bm{q _T=\bm{q}$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-196: Additional constraints includes zero initial/final joint velocity $\dot{\bm{q _0=\dot{\bm{q _T=0$, and robot dynamic and kinematic limits as follow: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \bm{q}_{\text{min}} \leq \bm{q}(t) \leq \bm{q}_{\text{max}}, \dot{\bm{q}}_{\text{min}} \leq \dot{\bm{q}}(t) \leq \dot{\bm{q}}_{\text{max}}, \ddot{\bm{q}}_{\text{min}} \leq \ddot{\bm{q}}(t) \leq \ddot{\bm{q}}_{\text{max}} \forall t \in [0, T]. \end{aligned}\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-197: Given a sequence of object-centric SE(3) perception poses, we leverage redundancy of the dual arms to optimize the joint configurations for fast execution.
elements.sent-2411-198: Secondly, we convert this formulation into a parallel multi-objectives problem.
elements.sent-2411-199: Finally, we train a differentiable time estimator to capture the execution time, which can directly incorporated in the objectives for optimization.
elements.sent-2411-200: In a dual-arm robot system, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:real_robot_experiment_setup , one robot, called the tool robot, holds the tool (camera or scanner), while the other arm, called the reference robot, holds the object to be modeled.}
elements.sent-2411-201: The concept of a relative Jacobian is built on vectors that represent the position and orientation of the \ac{TCP of the tool robot relative to the \ac{TCP of the reference robot.}}
elements.sent-2411-202: It imposes a constraint regarding the relative motion between the two \ac{TCP s without any restrictions regarding their absolute positions.}
elements.sent-2411-203: Using this Jacobian, the focus in a two-arm maneuver is the relative motion between the \ac{TCP .}
elements.sent-2411-204: The relative position vector $\bm{x _{R}$ is defined as: \begin{equation}\bm{x}_R = \bm{x}_B - \bm{x}_A\end{equation} where $\bm{x _A, \bm{x _B \in \mathbb{R ^{n_R}$ are the \ac{TCP pose of the reference robot and the tool robot, respectively and $n_R$ refers to the dimension of robot's workspace.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-205: By differentiating this equation with respect to time, we obtain: \begin{equation}\dot{\bm{x}}_R = \dot{\bm{x}}_B - \dot{\bm{x}}_A = J_R \dot{\bm{q}}\end{equation} where $\dot{\bm{x _R$ represents the relative velocity between two robots' \ac{TCP s and $\dot{\bm{x _B, \dot{\bm{x _A$ denote the absolute velocity of the respective robot's \ac{TCP .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-206: The term $n_{[\cdot]}$ denotes the dimension of the robot workspace or joint space and $n_T = n_A + n_B$ is the total number of \ac{DoF s for the system.}
elements.sent-2411-207: The primary objective for relative \ac{IK problem is to accurately determine the relative pose between the reference robot and the tool robot.}
elements.sent-2411-208: For the relative position, the objective function can be formulated as: \begin{equation}\chi_p(\bm{q}) = \| \bm{p}_{R,target} - (FK_B(\bm{q}_B) - FK_A(\bm{q}_A) \|_2^2 \label{eq:position}\end{equation} where $FK_A(\bm{q _A)$ and $FK_B(\bm{q _B)$ are the forward kinematics functions of robots A and B given their joint configurations, and $\bm{p _{R,target}$ is the relative target position of the robot B \ac{TCP with respect to the robot A \ac{TCP .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-209: For the relative orientation error, the objective function is given by: \begin{equation}\chi_o(\bm{q}) = \text{d}( q_{R,target}, \text{d}(q_B[FK(\bm{q}_B)], q_A[FK(\bm{q}_A)]) ), \label{eq:orientation}\end{equation} where $d(\cdot)$ is the displacement between two quatenions, $q_{R,target}$ is the relative target quaternion of the robot B relative to the robot A, and note that $\bm{q}$ refers joint configuration of the robot, and $q$ represents quaternions.
elements.sent-2411-210: To represent the motion time, a commonly used surrogate metric—also employed as a baseline in evaluations—is the distance between the starting and target joint configurations: \begin{equation}\chi_{\text{mt}}(\bm{q}) =\| \bm{q} - \bm{q}_0 \|_2^2,\end{equation} where $\bm{q}$ is the joint configuration being optimized.
elements.sent-2411-211: In this formulation, a bang-bang control strategy can be assumed at the velocity level, and the distance metric is normalized by introducing a weighting factor $w_i$ to account for the different velocity limits of each joint: \begin{equation}\chi_{\text{mt}}(\bm{q}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n_T} w_i \left( q_i - q_{i,0} \right)^2. \label{eq:distance}\end{equation} where $n_T$ represents total number of \ac{DoF s for the system.}
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{Figures/Pipeline_fixed.png} \caption{Pipeline of ETA-IK: given a desired perception pose, ETA-IK first generate a batch of initial configurations (yellow). A multilayer-perceptron time apporiximator are then integrated in a parallel optimization framework (blue). After $N$ iterations, the results is a batch of joint configurations. The best solution is selected according to different criteria (green).} \label{fig:pipeline2}
elements.sent-2411-212: Using surrogate loss such as distance between joint configurations as optimization objectives assumes that resultant \ac{PTP motion between the start and target configuration is collision-free and executable.}
elements.sent-2411-213: For a cluttered environment or a dual-arm setting, this assumption is usually not legitimate and therefore a post-processing step such as path planning is needed.
elements.sent-2411-214: This additional step introduce an gab between the distance-based surrogate loss and the actual execution time.
elements.sent-2411-215: Fig. \ref{fig:why_time_estimator shows the difference of using cyan dashed surrogate distance and the green actual feasible trajectory.}
elements.sent-2411-216: The actual trajectory is much longer and reflects a longer execution time.
elements.sent-2411-217: Furthermore, the robot's dynamic limitations, such as acceleration and jerk, also make surrogate distance metric impractical.
elements.sent-2411-218: Therefore, directly coping execution time in the optimization can provide a more timely efficient solution.
elements.sent-2411-219: For dual arms mounted on a fixed base, we can deploy offline optimal path planning or trajectory optimization algorithms to compute the collision-free motion between two arbitrary joint configurations and at the end acquire the actual execution time.
elements.sent-2411-220: Instead, we collect data from these offline methods and use a multilayer perceptron (MLP) $f_\theta$ to approximate the execution time during optimization.
elements.sent-2411-221: Previous works \cite{huang2024planning show that MLPs are capable of capturing the latent relation between joint configurations and time.}
elements.sent-2411-222: Dataset The time approximator estimates the execution time of a collision-free trajectory between two configurations.
elements.sent-2411-223: It is worth mentioning that different trajectory generators can produce massively different results for the same start-goal query.
elements.sent-2411-224: For these reasons, two different generators, TOPPRA \cite{pham_new_2017 and Curobo TrajOpt \cite{sundaralingam_curobo_2023 , are used to collect datasets for training the time estimator.}}
elements.sent-2411-225: The key distinction between these two trajectory generators is that the Curobo TrajOpt takes collision into account and always returns collision-free trajectories, whereas the TOPPRA only considers the joint limits constraints.
elements.sent-2411-226: The dataset includes 250,000 collision-free start-target pairs and the respective execution time of the trajectories generated by TOPPRA and Curobo TrajOpt.
elements.sent-2411-227: In the dataset, approximately 50\% of the trajectories generated by TOPPRA are not collision-free, noting that all collisions in this context refer to self-collisions or collisions with static obstacles.
elements.sent-2411-228: In these cases, the execution time provided by TOPPRA serves a similar role as the surrogate distance, but it is more accurate since the dynamic limitations are considered.
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{Figures/f4_rebuttal.pdf} \caption{Execution time approximator is able to implicitly encode the self-collision or static environment information when the dataset considered collision avoidance. Considering the collision, the green trajectory is much longer than simply connecting the start and end configurations, shown in cyan.} \label{fig:why_time_estimator}
elements.sent-2411-229: Training The MLP takes the start-goal pair as input and outputs the estimated time.
elements.sent-2411-230: In practice, we use positional encoding $\bm{q _{PE}$ = [$\bm{q}$, $\cos\bm{q}$, $\sin\bm{q}$] as input for the approximator.}
elements.sent-2411-231: Given the start and target configuration $\bm{q _0$, $\bm{q _T$, the approximator outputs \begin{equation}\chi_{\text{mt}}(\bm{q}, \bm{q}_0) = f_\theta(\bm{q}_{PE,0}, \bm{q}_{PE,T}).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-232: Both input and output is normalized to the range [-1, 1] based on the statistics of the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-233: It is worth mentioning that it is important that the learned MLP can correctly recolonize which start-goal pair has less execution time and provide the gradient of it.
elements.sent-2411-234: The absolute value of the estimation is not crucial in the use case.
elements.sent-2411-235: Throughout the training, mean square error (MSE) loss between the predicted $\chi_{\text{mt (\bm{q , \bm{q _0)$ and the ground truth in the dataset is used.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-236: The hyperparamters such as learning rate, numbers of the latent embedding are determined by a sweep process using Bayesian optimization.
elements.sent-2411-237: Inference In the inference phase, namely the optimization phase, the trained MLP takes new configurations of every iteration as input and estimate the time.
elements.sent-2411-238: Since the optimization is a Quasi-Newton based method.
elements.sent-2411-239: The gradients of the MLP regarding the inputs are used for the optimization.
elements.sent-2411-240: MLPs can not only achieve fast inference time during optimization, but also support batch operations and provide gradients for the optimization.
elements.sent-2411-241: Inverse kinematics optimization aimed at achieving global optimality is computationally expensive.
elements.sent-2411-242: A recent study \cite{votroubek_globally_2024 reports an average computation time of 77.6 seconds to optimize a system with 10 DoF using the similar objective functions in \ref{subsec: multi-obj-ik .}}
elements.sent-2411-243: Due to the curse of dimensionality, applying this method for online \ac{IK is impractical.}
elements.sent-2411-244: The entire objective function is \begin{equation}f(\bm{q}) = w_p \cdot \chi_p(\bm{q}) + w_o \cdot\chi_o(\bm{q}) + w_{mt} \cdot \chi_{\text{mt}}(\bm{q}, \bm{q}_0)+ w_b \cdot \chi_{b}(\bm{q})\end{equation} where $w_i$ represent the weight for the corresponding objective terms, and $\chi_{b (\bm{q )$ is the joint limit cost proposed in \cite{sundaralingam_curobo_2023 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-245: Since the proposed method also consider the motion time, the metric for selecting the optimal solution can be defined in several ways: it can prioritize the pose error, the motion time, or a combination of both.
elements.figure-2411-010: \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.5} \setlength{\aboverulesep}{0pt} \setlength{\belowrulesep}{0pt} \newcommand{\est}[0]{{\color{blue} Est. Time }} \newcommand{\srr}[0]{{\color{purple} Surrogate Dist. }} \begin{tabular}{cl|c|c|c|c|c|c|c} Pick-IK-1 Pick-IK-256 \multicolumn{2}{c|}{ETA-IK-20} \multicolumn{2}{c|}{ETA-IK-2k } ETA-IK-200k \srr \est \srr \est \est Execution Time [s] 2.347 - 2.073 2.055 1.963 \textbf{1.926} 1.892 Joint Configuration Dist. [$rad^2$] 5.50 - 4.71 4.73 4.33 4.33 4.28 Computation Time [ms] 15.78 833.46 59.40 95.77 62.48 99.70 980.23 IK Success Rate [\%] 99.5 0 100 100 100 100 100 \end{tabular} \caption{\label{tab:exp_3} Evaluation results based on random sampling from 10,000 IK instances. Two variants of optimization objectives for ETA-IK are evaluated. While both of the variants use relative pose, \srr refers to the variant using joint configuration distance loss in the objective function and \est indicates the variant directly coping with the trained time approximator. Bold values highlight that, even with similar joint distances, the execution time approximator identifies more optimal solutions. Italic values show that increasing the number of optimization seeds further improves execution time. Underlined values indicate that multi-threaded GPU inference ensures scalability without increasing computation time. }
elements.sent-2411-246: Initially, we configured a simple simulation environment featuring a dual Franka setup without environmental collisions to evaluate the general performance of the proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-247: This setup involves generating a random, self-collision-free initial joint configuration $\bm{q _0$ and a random relative Cartesian pose $\bm{x _{R,target}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-248: For comparative analysis, we use Pick-IK (Reimplementation of Bio-IK \cite{8449979 by MoveIt2) as the baseline.}
elements.sent-2411-249: The optimization includes joint configuration distance as an additional metric.
elements.sent-2411-250: We also evaluate multi-threaded computation on the CPU, which is provided as a built-in functionality of Pick-IK.
elements.sent-2411-252: The trajectory execution time after post-processing with TOPPRA.
elements.sent-2411-253: The difference between the initial joint configuration and the computed solution.
elements.sent-2411-254: The computation time required to solve the \ac{IK problems.}
elements.sent-2411-255: The success rate of solving the \ac{IK problems.}
elements.sent-2411-256: For Pick-IK, the default setup with a single seed and CPU multi-threading with 256 seeds is selected as the baseline.
elements.sent-2411-257: The global mode is used for the \ac{PTP \ac{IK problem, and the \ac{IK solver timeout is set to $0.5$ seconds.}}}
elements.sent-2411-258: The weight for the joint displacement is set to $0.002$, as higher values result in an extremely high failure rate.
elements.sent-2411-259: For ETA-IK, the default parameter values of Curobo \cite{sundaralingam_curobo_2023 are used.}
elements.sent-2411-260: The joint configuration distance weights are set to $5$ and $20$ for the MPPI and Newton methods.
elements.sent-2411-261: The learned approximator applies displacement weights of $5$ and $50$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-262: The experiments were conducted on a desktop PC with an Intel i9 processor and an NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU.
elements.sent-2411-263: The execution time approximator was also trained locally on this machine, requiring approximately 1 hour.
elements.sent-2411-264: Statistical results were evaluated over 10,000 independent inverse kinematics queries.
elements.sent-2411-265: Depending on the trajectory generation method used, the dataset generation time for 250,000 trajectories ranged from several hours to approximately one day.
elements.sent-2411-266: In general, \ac{IK solutions obtained from the parallel multi-objective optimization framework result in shorter execution times.}
elements.sent-2411-267: Even when the joint differences are identical, the execution time approximator can identify a more efficient solution for execution (see bold results in Table \ref{tab:exp_3 ).}
elements.sent-2411-268: Additionally, increasing the number of optimization seeds further improves the solution quality (see italicized results in Table \ref{tab:exp_3 ).}
elements.sent-2411-269: Although the proposed method generally exhibits longer computation times compared to Pick-IK, and the execution time approximator incurs additional inference overhead, it is important to note that the computation time remains unchanged when using 20 or 2000 seeds due to the multi-threading capabilities of the GPU (see underlined results in Table \ref{tab:exp_3 ).}
elements.sent-2411-270: In contrast, Pick-IK with 256 threads experiences a significant computational overhead compared to its single-threaded counterpart.
elements.sent-2411-271: Moreover, the success rate of Pick-IK with 256 threads is zero, as all computations exceed the predefined time limit.
elements.sent-2411-272: While the initial evaluation benchmarks the proposed method against baseline methods and standard metrics, the subsequent ablation study investigates how different design choices within the proposed framework affect performance, particularly in terms of shorter execution times and implicit collision handling.
elements.sent-2411-273: The real experimental setup for the dual-arm system, shown in Fig. \ref{fig:real_robot_experiment_setup , consists of a UR5 and a KUKA LBR iiwa 14 R820 robot, with a combined total of 13 \ac{DoF .}}
elements.sent-2411-274: The KUKA iiwa is designated for object grasping due to its higher load capacity, while a high-resolution 3D LiDAR Robin W from Seyond is mounted on the UR5's \ac{EE for scanning purposes.}
elements.sent-2411-275: In the simulation, additional static obstacles, such as the table, LiDAR, and viewpoint sphere, are present compared to the environment described in Section \ref{sec: General Evaluation (as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:simulation ).}}
elements.sent-2411-276: To implement absolute \ac{TCP pose \ac{IK , we sample a random, collision-free initial joint configuration $\bm{q _0$ and a random reference target joint configuration $\bm{q _{target}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-277: The relative Cartesian pose $\bm{x _{R,target}$ and the absolute \ac{TCP poses $\bm{x _{A,target , \bm{x _{B,target}$ of the two robots are then computed individually.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-278: The robot joint limits used when generating the dataset for the network align with those in TOPPRA, including velocity $\left\{3.15, 3.15, 3.15, 3.2, 3.2, 3.2\right\}$ and $\left\{10.0, 10.0, 10.0, 10.0, 10.0, 10.0, 10.0\right\}$ $\frac{rad {s}$, acceleration $\left\{5.0, 5.0, 3.0, 2.0, 2.0, 2.0\right\}, \left\{5.0, 5.0, 3.0, 2.0, 2.0, 2.0, 2.0 \right\}$ $\frac{rad {s^{2 }$ constraints for UR5 and IIWA respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-279: The maximum jerk for all the joints is $500\frac{rad {s^{3 }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-280: For the ablation study, different design choices are varied for evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-281: Methods (A) and (C) optimize Eq. (\ref{eq:distance ), using absolute and relative Cartesian poses as the surrogate execution time term in the objective function.}
elements.sent-2411-282: Method (B) employs the trained execution time approximator but considers absolute Cartesian poses.
elements.sent-2411-283: Methods (D), (E), and (F) use the trained execution time approximator with different solution selection criteria, namely best time, best cost, and best pose error.
elements.sent-2411-284: To explore the optimal solution, the number of initial guesses and optimization iterations are set at a relatively high value, namely $4096$ initial guesses and $120$ iterations.
elements.sent-2411-285: This ensures a comprehensive evaluation of the solution space while maintaining computation time within a practically acceptable range.
elements.sent-2411-286: The weights for position and orientation error are $2000, 2500$ and the execution time weights are set to be $250$ for joint distance and $500$ for execution time approximator.

elements.sent-2411-287: Videos demonstrating the system’s performance are available on the \url{https://yucheng-tang.github.io/eta-ik-website/ .}
elements.sent-2411-288: Unlike traditional trajectory generation techniques or physics-based simulators, ETA-IK leverages a trained execution time approximator that can be efficiently integrated into the optimization framework.
elements.sent-2411-289: This is enabled by its ability to support parallel inference and to provide differentiable outputs.
elements.sent-2411-290: Experimental results demonstrated that incorporating motion execution time directly into the IK optimization framework yields significant improvements in efficiency compared to traditional methods that rely on surrogate metrics, such as joint configuration distance.
elements.sent-2411-291: By incorporating collision avoidance into the optimization process, our approach could account for self- and static collision without introducing substantial computational overhead.
elements.sent-2411-292: In addition, we identified a limitation of the proposed method: the additional inference time required by the neural network-based execution time approximator compared to simple distance-based surrogate metrics.
elements.sent-2411-293: However, this computational overhead remains nearly constant as long as the number of parallel threads does not exceed the capacity of the GPU.
elements.sent-2411-294: Nevertheless, it should be acknowledged that the proposed approach relies on access to a sufficiently powerful GPU to support efficient parallel inference and optimization within practical time constraints.
elements.sent-2411-295: Future research may explore reducing the overhead of the approximator further and learning more effective initial pose distributions—potentially replacing Halton sampling—with methods such as the one proposed in \cite{huang_diffusionseeder_2024 .. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}}
